plant.description: Unique History: Classified under the 'Upavisha' (semi-toxic) group in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. The name 'Kupilu' literally translates to 'one that stays in the hand of Shiva' (referring to neurological potency). It is noted as the 'Strychnine Tree' in Western clinical medicine. Mentioned in the Vedas and classical Sanskrit literature for its unmatched 'Vatavyadhi' (Vata-balancing) property. Physical Characteristics: A medium-to-large evergreen tree growing up to 10-15 meters in height, with a majestic crown and dark-grey, rough bark. The leaves are opposite, ovate-elliptic, and leathery. The flowers are small, greenish-white, terminal cymes. The fruit is a globose berry (orange when ripe) containing flat, disk-shaped, silky-grey seeds. It is found throughout India and tropical regions worldwide (especially East and South India). Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Indole Alkaloids (Strychnine, Brucine). The presence of strychnine (1-2% in seeds) is crucial for its neuro-stimulant, anti-viral, and anti-tubercular effects (when appropriately purified). Strict Standardizations: The seed is standardized in API based on its greyish-white color and characteristic extremely bitter-biting taste. Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<2%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>15%). HPLC quantification is used to monitor 1% to 2% strychnine in commercial extracts. Purification Process (Shodhana): The raw seeds are traditionally soaked in 'Gomutra' (cow's urine) or 'Dugdha' (milk) for seven days followed by washing with hot water and frying in Ghee (Ghrita) to remove excessive toxic alkaloids before medicinal use.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_karma: Powerful Vata and Kapha Hara (pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas). Due to its Tikta (bitter) and Katu (pungent) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Shulaprashamana' (analgesic) and 'Vatavyadhi' (neurological disorders) therapies using its 'Vyavayi' (swift) and 'Tikshna' (sharp) potency. It may significantly aggravate Pitta if used in excess.

purified_seed_powder__shuddha_.method: 60 mg to 125 mg (extremely low dose) of purified powder taken once daily with honey or warm milk for systemic detoxification and nerve support.

agnitundi_vati.method: Classical tablet form used for chronic indigestion and malabsorption; taken (125-250mg) after or during meals for immediate relief.

vishatinduka_vati.method: Medicated tablet used primarily for nerve pain and paralysis support; taken (125mg) daily under expert care.

external_paste.method: Ground purified seed or paste applied directly to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, joint pain) to reduce heat and swelling.

medicated_oil__taila_.method: Seeds or root processed in sesame oil; used for local scalp massage (hair growth) and joint pain (Shothahara).

dosage.children: Strictly NOT recommended for children under any circumstances due to high toxicity.
dosage.adults: Powder (Purified): 60-125 mg; Tablet: 125-250 mg; Oil: External Application only (usually), divided throughout the day before meals.
dosage.elderly: Very low doses (30-60mg) are advisable initially, monitoring for any increase in dizziness or intestinal cramping.

medicinal_uses: Expert Neurological and Nerve Pain Specialist: Kupilu is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all conditions of neurological pain and paralysis. Its 'Tikshna' (sharp) and 'Ushna' (heating) nature provided by strychnine alkaloids helps in stimulating the nervous system and providing systemic stabilization (Vedanasthapana). WHO-aligned research recognizes its potent neuro-stimulant and anti-inflammatory action when purified.

Paralysis and Stroke recovery: Historically known as 'Pakshaghatahara' (paralysis-reliever). It effectively improves motor function and physical recovery from stroke by stimulating the nerve-terminals and clearing 'Ama' (toxins) from the channels (Srotas).

Digestive Fire and Metabolism: Its Tikshna (sharp) and Ushna (heating) nature helps in igniting the Jatharagni (digestive fire) and cleared stagnant food from the gut. It is traditionally used in 'Agnitundi Vati' for chronic indigestion and malabsorption.

Pain and Abdominal Colic: Known as 'Shulaprashamana' (pain-relieving). It is traditionally used to provide relief from abdominal cramps, flatulence, and bloating by clearing 'Vata-avarana' (Vata-obstruction) in the gut.

Rheumatic and Joint Pain: Its systemic anti-inflammatory (Shothahara) properties help in reducing localized pain and stiffness in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata), sciatica, and chronic musculoskeletal pain.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in Indole Alkaloids (Strychnine, Brucine), Loganin, and various Glycosides. Strychnine is the primary bioactive responsible for its potent anti-Parkinsonian, libido-enhancing, and neuro-stimulant effects (when purified).

safety_profile.warning: GRAS Status: NOT classified as GRAS. It is a potent semi-poison (Upavisha) and must only be used by qualified Ayurvedic physicians. National and International regulations apply to its collection and sale.
safety_profile.medical_supervision: Use under the guidance of a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner.
contraindications: Strictly contraindicated in individuals with high gastric sensitivity, and during pregnancy (abortifacient risk in high doses). Toxicity is extreme; non-purified use or overdose is lethal.
possible_side_effects: Overdose or non-purified use results in muscle spasms, projectile vomiting, respiratory failure, and death. Discontinue if extreme bitterness causes nausea.
drug_interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-epileptic or sedative medications as it may potentiate their effects (convulsant risk). No severe adverse interactions reported in clinical literature with purified doses.

plant_profile.note: AI estimated profile based on known properties
